title: Testing with pytest
subtitle: JUnit patterns → pytest patterns, with less ceremony

sections.java: // JUnit 5: annotations + assertion methods import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*; class DiscountTest { @Test void appliesPercentage() { assertEquals(90.0, discount(100, 0.1)); } }
sections.python: # pytest: plain functions + plain assert from pricing import discount def test_applies_percentage(): assert discount(100, 0.1) == 90.0 # No class, no annotations, # no special assert methods

sections.title: Plain assert — No assertEquals Zoo
sections.content: pytest rewrites the bare assert statement to produce rich failure output, so you never memorize assertEquals / assertTrue / assertThat. assert result == expected shows both values on failure. Test functions are just functions whose names start with test_; no test class, no @Test required.

sections.java: // Parameterized tests need annotations + source @ParameterizedTest @CsvSource({"2,3,5", "0,0,0", "-1,1,0"}) void adds(int a, int b, int sum) { assertEquals(sum, add(a, b)); }
sections.python: # One decorator, a list of tuples import pytest @pytest.mark.parametrize("a,b,total", [ (2, 3, 5), (0, 0, 0), (-1, 1, 0), ]) def test_adds(a, b, total): assert add(a, b) == total

sections.java: // Expecting an exception assertThrows(PaymentError.class, () -> { charge(-5); }); // Setup with @BeforeEach @BeforeEach void setup() { db = new FakeDb(); }
sections.python: # Expecting an exception with pytest.raises(PaymentError): charge(-5) # Setup via a fixture (dependency injected) @pytest.fixture def db(): return FakeDb()

sections.title: Fixtures Are Dependency Injection for Tests
sections.content: A pytest fixture is a function that provides a resource; any test that wants it just names it as a parameter: def test_save(db):. pytest matches the name and injects the value — the same DI idea as Spring's @Autowired in tests, but with zero configuration. Fixtures can yield to run teardown after the test, replacing @AfterEach.

sections.java: // Mockito for mocks/stubs PaymentGateway gw = mock(PaymentGateway.class); when(gw.charge(any())).thenReturn(true); verify(gw).charge(100);
sections.python: # unittest.mock — built into the stdlib from unittest.mock import MagicMock gw = MagicMock() gw.charge.return_value = True process(gw) gw.charge.assert_called_with(100)

sections.title: Test File & Function Naming Is Discovery, Not Decoration
sections.content: pytest finds tests by name convention, not annotations: files test_*.py (or *_test.py), functions test_*, optional classes Test* (no __init__). Name a test function check_total instead of test_total and pytest silently skips it — a quiet trap when you're used to @Test making intent explicit.

sections.question: How does pytest know that a function is a test to run?
options: It must live in a class extending TestCase
By naming convention — the function name starts with test_ in a test_*.py file
Every function in the file is run as a test
sections.explanation: pytest uses discovery by convention: it collects functions named test_* in files named test_*.py (or *_test.py). There's no @Test annotation and no required base class. The flip side is a gotcha — misname the function and it's silently skipped.
